perturbation.original_text:
4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR DOES NOT MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT.
4.2.1 LICENSOR SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, NON-INFRINGEMENT AND TITLE, AND ALL OTHER WARRANTIES THAT MAY OTHERWISE ARISE FROM COURSE OF DEALING, USAGE OF TRADE OR CUSTOM.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 LIMITATION OF WARRANTY AND LIABILITY. LICENSOR DOES NOT MAKE WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, RELATED TO OR ARISING OUT OF THE LICENSED MARK OR THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific disclaimer of implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose, the contract appears to offer these warranties where, by the original text, it did not. This creates ambiguity. California law implies a warranty of merchantability in many commercial transactions. Removing the disclaimer without any replacement could lead to a consumer or licensee believing a warranty exists when it was intended that it did not, potentially violating consumer protection laws related to misleading or deceptive practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2316 specifies the requirements for excluding or modifying warranties of merchantability. While the original text followed these requirements, removing the specific disclaimer implies the potential existence of these warranties, creating ambiguity about the actual terms of the agreement, thus making it deceptive.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2316. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this division on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subdivision (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that “There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof.” (3) Notwithstanding subdivision (2) (a) Unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like “as is,” “with all faults” or other language w
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific disclaimer of implied warranties, which creates a legal contradiction by potentially implying these warranties exist where they were originally intended not to. This directly relates to California Commercial Code § 2316, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which outlines the requirements for excluding or modifying warranties of merchantability. The original text complied with these requirements by explicitly disclaiming such warranties. Removing the disclaimer without replacement introduces ambiguity and potentially misleads consumers, undermining the legal standard for warranty exclusion.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Indemnification. Licensed Users, at Licensed Users' own expense, shall indemnify, hold harmless and defend Licensor, its affiliates, successors and assigns, and its and their directors, officers, employees and agents, against any claim, demand, cause of action, debt, expense or liability (including attorneys' fees and costs), to the extent that the foregoing (a) is based on a claim resulting solely from any service provided or offered by Licensed Users, (b) results from a material breach, or is based on a claim that, if true, would be a material breach, of this Agreement by Licensed Users, or (c) is based upon Licensed Users' unauthorized or improper use of the Licensed Mark.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Indemnification. Licensed Users, at Licensed Users' own expense, shall indemnify, hold harmless and defend Licensor, its affiliates, successors and assigns, and its and their directors, officers, employees and agents, against any claim, demand, cause of action, debt, expense or liability (including attorneys' fees and costs).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the conditions under which the Licensed Users should indemnify, we make the clause very broad and subject to misinterpretation. California law places limits on indemnity agreements, especially concerning a party's own negligence or willful misconduct. Removing the limiting phrases could be interpreted as requiring the Licensee to indemnify the Licensor even for the Licensor's own actions, which may be unenforceable or even void under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2782 generally prohibits indemnity agreements that purport to indemnify a party against its own sole negligence or willful misconduct in construction contracts. Though this is a trademark agreement and not a construction contract, it still illustrates the public policy against broad indemnity clauses that shift liability unfairly. The modified clause, lacking specific limitations, could be challenged as violating this public policy, creating a legal contradiction.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2782. (a) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, 2782.5, and 2782.6, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any construction contract and that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages for death or bodily injury to persons, injury to property, or any other loss, damage or expense arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee or the promisee’s agents, servants, or independent contractors who are directly responsible to the promisee, or for defects in design furnished by those persons, are against public policy and are void and unenforceable; provided, however, that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers’ compensation, or agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the Insurance Code. (b) (1) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, and 2782.5, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affectin
perturbation.justification: The modification to the indemnification clause, by removing the specific conditions under which Licensed Users must indemnify Licensor, creates a potentially unenforceable obligation. According to the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, this could extend to situations where Licensor is solely negligent or engages in willful misconduct. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code § 2782(a), states that provisions in construction contracts purporting to indemnify a party against liability for damages arising from their sole negligence or willful misconduct are against public policy and void. Although this is a trademark agreement and not a construction contract, the Law Explanation correctly points out the underlying public policy against broad indemnity clauses. The original contract limited the scope of indemnity; removing those limits creates a contradiction with the public policy reflected in the law.

perturbation.original_text: 4.10 Governing Law and Jurisdiction. The provisions of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California (excluding any conflict of law rule or principle that would refer to the laws of another jurisdiction). Each Party hereto irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in California, in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and each Party hereby irrevocably agrees that all claims in respect of any such action or proceeding must be brought and/or defended in any such court; provided, however, that matters which are under the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts shall be brought in the Federal District Court for the Central District of California. Each Party hereto consents to service of process by any means authorized by the applicable law of the forum in any action brought under or arising out of this Agreement, and each Party irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent each may effectively do so, the defense of an inconvenient forum to the maintenance of such action or proceeding in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.10 Governing Law and Jurisdiction. The provisions of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California (excluding any conflict of law rule or principle that would refer to the laws of another jurisdiction). Each Party consents to service of process by any means authorized by the applicable law of the forum in any action brought under or arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause where "Each Party hereto irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in California, in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and each Party hereby irrevocably agrees that all claims in respect of any such action or proceeding must be brought and/or defended in any such court; provided, however, that matters which are under the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal courts shall be brought in the Federal District Court for the Central District of California. Each Party hereto irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent each may effectively do so, the defense of an inconvenient forum to the maintenance of such action or proceeding in any such court." we create an opening to challenge the jurisdiction of the courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 establishes the rules for venue in federal courts. While parties can consent to jurisdiction, omitting the explicit consent clause from the contract opens the door for a party to argue that venue is improper under § 1391, potentially leading to litigation over jurisdiction and contradicting the intent of the original clause to establish a clear forum for disputes.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified jurisdiction and venue in California courts, including federal courts in the Central District. The modified contract removes this clause, which, according to the Law Explanation, opens the door for a party to argue that venue is improper under 28 U.S. Code § 1391. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 28 USC 1391, confirms this code governs venue in federal district courts. By omitting the jurisdiction clause, the contract potentially contradicts the intent of establishing a clear forum, making it easier for a party to challenge venue based on the general provisions of § 1391.
